title: Living in Boulder vlog 1 - 1 - 2017: Google Earthing Rome

enrichment.summary: Kyle gives a late-night vlog update on his hobby project of recreating Rome (and the broader Roman Empire) in Google Earth/a 3D mapping tool, describing his process of tracing buildings, modeling apartment blocks from historical maps, and a side-track of building a scaled 'Boulder Rome' layout. He also mentions plans to count individual apartments in region 14 of ancient Rome using the building geometry he's created.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: Thinks he doesn't need to travel to tell the story of Rome — keeping the format consistent is enough
Believes it's feasible to count individual apartments from the building geometry he's traced, something he previously thought too difficult

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: His tongue hurts because he's been sticking it in his nails too much.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: He was working on the region 14 trans type variable in Rome but got sidetracked.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: He Googled the tiger river from the mouth to the stream turn-off to see where the Romans got people to help restart Rome.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: The area around the river was about 56 kilometers.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: He got sidetracked again and started working on his Boulder Rome project.

claims.subject: Boulder Rome
claims.claim: It involves rebuilding the Roman Empire in Boulder, not just Rome but the entire empire and other historical elements like castles and battles.

claims.subject: Boulder Rome
claims.claim: He believes you can change the scene while keeping the format the same for the same experience.

claims.subject: Boulder Rome
claims.claim: He created the whole Roman Empire in Boulder, including the Mediterranean Sea, Black Sea, and islands.

claims.subject: Boulder Rome
claims.claim: The coastline in his project is almost 20 kilometers long.

claims.subject: Boulder Rome
claims.claim: He filled in a giant lake with islands on top of cities.

claims.subject: Rome project
claims.claim: He finished region 14, which had a map, but thinks he might have been doing it wrong by counting apartment buildings instead of copying whole blocks.

claims.subject: Rome project
claims.claim: He plans to copy and paste whole blocks from the map for the rest of Rome.

claims.subject: Rome project
claims.claim: He is finishing the buildings he already traced on the map.

claims.subject: Rome project
claims.claim: He started with a type of route in 2014, which motivated him to work on the rest of Rome.

claims.subject: Rome project
claims.claim: He did a few temples and gardens that look like a cartoon, with everything visible from the top.

claims.subject: Rome project
claims.claim: He believes he can get all the buildings done in a week.

claims.subject: Rome project
claims.claim: On original region 14, he built up to five color-coded, transparent levels.

claims.subject: Rome project
claims.claim: He thought about counting all the apartments individually, some of which are three-room apartments.

claims.subject: Rome project
claims.claim: He might be able to count all the apartments in Rome using the math from the buildings.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: He is too lazy to create a visual explanation of his project right now.

transcript.text: Hello, living in Boulder, Fulog. It's 1.35 a.m. on January the 2nd right now.
So, my tongue kind of hurts. I've been sticking fingernails in it too much.
I don't normally do that.
Okay, so, last time I did a video, I was working on Region 14, Trans-Tiberium in Rome.
But then I got sidetracked. I think it was a couple days since I did a video.
I Google Earthed, drew in, and Google Earthed the Tiber River from the mouth of the river
To all the way up to the little stream turnoff to Curace,
Where the Romans got the people to help Rome when it was restarted.
So, it was kind of like the area of Rome. It's like 56 kilometers long.
Yeah, so then at that point, I started thinking, and so I got sidetracked,
And I started working on my Boulder Rome.
I'm Google Earthed Rome, but not just Rome, but all the Rome, like the whole Roman Empire.
And a lot of other things in history, like castles and battles.
I want to do videos in old towns, all the ancient towns with their walls.
But, yeah, I'm also doing Boulder, all these different times.
And so, I wanted to do Boulder Rome.
So, that's rebuilding Rome on Boulder, so that when I'm listening to the story of Rome, I'm in Boulder.
I mean, I don't have to go to Rome.
I can stay here.
And I'm reliving, because I don't think you have to.
I think you can change the kind of the scene, but if you keep the format, it's the same experience.
So, you can work creating Rome here, even though, you know, the shapes are different.
So, yeah, I recreated the whole Roman Empire.
I didn't, you know, totally do it before.
But I suddenly had a eureka moment that I should put the Mediterranean Sea in America.
So, I was kind of wondering how I was going to deal with all of the, you know, the islands.
Yeah, so I put it in, and it fits right in the Mediterranean Sea, Black Sea, and then Sea of Azole next to it fit right in America.
Like an inland lake.
And the, you know, the coastline is only 20 kilometers that way.
You know, if it's going to be the same as Rome.
And that's like Bonne Mutt.
I want to go to the tops of the mountains and look at it and see if you can see the ocean and see the islands up there.
But, yeah, so then different, you know, within the giant lake, all the different islands I put on top of cities, they're here.
So, yeah, so that was my little sidetrack of this last night.
Then today I was doing, so when I came back to work on Rome again, I was like, I think, I think I finished 3 to 14, just this one part that had the map on it.
And the apartments, and I thought to myself, maybe I was doing it wrong with the other parts.
I should be, instead of just, like, counting different, you know, apartment buildings, just throwing stuff on there.
I should just copy and paste whole blocks, you know, and big buildings, actual buildings from the map that I have for the other, the rest of Rome.
So I'll do that.
But, yeah, so now I'm working on, before I do the other parts of Rome, though, I'm actually finally finishing the buildings that I already traced that are on the map.
So that's really fun.
I started with Tiber Island, because that was in Region 14, and that got me motivated to do, yeah, the rest of Rome.
So I did a few temples and gardens, and it looks really good.
It looks like it's just like a cartoon.
And you can see in the top the way that I have it, so you can see everything, all the rooms.
It's really cool.
So, yeah, so I could do a lot just in a week.
So, yeah, here's my week.
Yesterday, I guess.
Yeah, we'll start it, you know, Monday.
I think yesterday, yeah, I started today, but pretty much at the beginning of the week.
So, yeah, we'll make it easier for me to remember when I started.
So, yeah, maybe in a week I can get this, all of the buildings just done and looking good.
And then, yeah, and then do the rest of the city so it's consistent.
Because on Region 14, I actually built it up to five levels.
And then I color-coded the levels and made them a little bit transparent so you can see them, each one, and you see which one they are.
And then I was thinking about, yeah, I actually did a count.
You can count all of the apartments individually.
And some are bigger than others.
It's really cool.
Most of them are one-rooms, but some of them are, like, three-room apartments, just like a normal modern one.
And so I would count it as one because you can see how, you know, it's just one apartment.
So, yeah, I might be able to count all the people, all the apartments in Rome with the map that it looks, that it actually is, I guess.
How it actually looks from the buildings, the map that I have.
It's not too hard to count.
Before, I was thinking it was too difficult.
So, yeah, I think I will try that.
